b: Flashcards Spaced repetition over the glossary, the symbol guide, and every quiz question you've missed. Cards you find hard come back sooner; cards you know retreat into the future. loading cards… Show the answer Again (soon) Got it (later) Easy (much later)
Glossary Every term on this site, in the same order we'd explain it in person: the plain-English version first, the precise definition second.

t: Bass Tutor — bass guitar & music theory, in plain language
b: Bass guitar & music theory, in plain language From "which one is the E string?" to walking through jazz changes — one interactive page at a time. Every idea starts with everyday words, every example is playable, and every page ends with something concrete to practice. Welcome back How this site works Each page: a plain-English explanation, something to hear and poke at (fretboards and notation you can tap — sound starts after your first tap), the theory in unfoldable layers, practice exercises with a log, and a short quiz. Use ← → arrow keys to move between pages. The path UNIT 0 Start here How to use this site, what gear you need, getting in tune, and an honest guide to picking your starting point. UNIT 1 soon Reading music The staff & bass clef · rhythm & counting · tab ↔ notation · time signatures — every example playable. UNIT 2 soon The fretboard Strings & fret math · notes on the fretboard · intervals as shapes · positions & fingering. UNIT 3 soon Theory core Intervals · the major scale · keys & the circle of fifths · minor scales · pentatonics · triads · 7th chords · diatonic harmony. UNIT 4 soon Technique Right hand · left hand · timing & groove · tone & dynamics · how to practice. UNIT 5 soon Bass lines The bassist's job · roots & chord tones · approach notes · walking bass 101 · the 12-bar blues · song forms & charts. UNIT 6 soon Styles Rock · blues shuffle · funk · soul/Motown · reggae · jazz walking — playable grooves for each. UNIT 7 soon Practice labs Fretboard trainer · ear trainer · groove machine · tuner · routine builder. UNIT 8 soon Expert track Modes · extensions · harmonic analysis · transcription · improvisation · slap & advanced techniques. Reference pages, all live as the site grows: the glossary (every term, plain English first), the symbol guide (every music symbol — searchable by descriptions like "the fancy F"), the concept map , search (press / anywhere), and flashcards (spaced repetition over terms, symbols, and the quiz questions you m

The concept map Every page on the site and how the ideas feed each other — colored by your progress. The map fills in as the units are published. Under construction The map appears here once the units are in place. Until then, start from the home page — Unit 0 is live.

How to read the symbols Every music symbol used on this site: its name, how to say it out loud, and what it means for a bass player. Search by name, by meaning, or by description — "flat", "the fancy F", and "squiggle that means rest" all work. Loading… (needs the dev server: npm run dev )
Search Pages, glossary terms, symbols, and practice exercises — plain phrasing works: try "two frets up", "swing feel", or "the fancy F". Results

t: Welcome — how to use this site
b: Unit 0 · Start Here Welcome — how to use this site This is a bass course that runs entirely in your browser: fretboards you can tap, notation that plays itself, a metronome, ear training, and a path that goes from "which one is the E string?" to reading real charts. What this site believes Three things, and everything else follows from them: 1. Plain words first. Every idea starts in everyday language, and the official term arrives only after you already understand the thing it names. When we say "notes that are two frets apart", you'll learn later that musicians call that a whole step — and by then it's just a new name for something you already know. Any term with a dotted underline is tappable and will explain itself. 2. Your ears and hands come along. Music theory that you can't hear is trivia. Every example on this site is playable — tap a fret diagram and it sounds, press ▶ under a line of notation and it plays. And every page ends with something concrete to practice on a real bass, with a starting tempo and a target. 3. Small, honest steps. Each page teaches one idea, checks it with a short quiz (asked in plain words, not vocabulary), and tells you where it leads. Quiz questions you miss turn into flashcards automatically, so the things you personally find hard come back around. How a page works Every topic page has the same flow: The idea — a plain-English explanation, usually with an everyday analogy. Hear it / find it — an interactive diagram or playable notation. Sound always starts with your tap — nothing plays on its own. The details — collapsible layers. Open as much as you like; you can follow the whole course without ever opening the deepest layer. Practice — exercises with notation and tab , a ▶ button so you know what they should sound like, tempo targets, and a "log practice" button that remembers what you've worked on. A quiz — a few questions to let you claim the page. Finishing the quiz marks the page complete on the concept map; it never blocks

How high or low a sound is. The perceived highness or lowness of a sound, set by its vibration frequency — faster vibration sounds higher. On bass, pressing closer to the body raises the pitch; thicker strings sit lower.
One named musical sound — like A, C♯, or E♭. A pitch with a name. Music uses twelve note names that repeat as you go higher or lower: A, A♯/B♭, B, C, C♯/D♭, D, D♯/E♭, E, F, F♯/G♭, G, G♯/A♭.

The four steel wires you pluck; each is named after the note it plays open. A 4-string bass in standard tuning has strings E–A–D–G, from thickest (lowest) to thinnest (highest). "The A string" means the second-thickest string, which sounds the note A when played open.
Playing a string without pressing any fret. A string plucked while no finger holds it down anywhere. Open strings sound each string's tuned note (E, A, D, or G) and are written as fret 0 in tab.

The metal strips along the neck; each one raises the note by one small step. The metal bars set into the fingerboard. Pressing a string just behind a fret shortens its vibrating length; each fret raises the pitch by one semitone (half step). "5th fret" means the space just behind the fifth metal bar counting from the headstock.

The same note, higher or lower — like two people singing the same tune, one deeper. The distance between a note and the next note with the same name (12 semitones). Notes an octave apart vibrate in a 2:1 ratio and sound like "the same note, higher/lower". On bass: two strings up and two frets over.

One fret's worth of distance — the smallest step in Western music. The smallest interval in common Western music: the distance from one fret to the next on the same string. Twelve semitones make an octave.
Two frets' worth of distance. An interval of two semitones — two frets on the same string. Scales are usually described as patterns of whole and half steps.

The distance between two notes. The gap in pitch between two notes, counted in scale steps and given names like "perfect fifth" or "minor third". Intervals are the units bass lines are built from — on the fretboard each interval is a repeatable shape.

The home note a chord or scale is built on — the note that gives it its name. The foundational note of a chord or scale: the C in C major, the A in Am7. The bassist's first job in most music is to sound the root when the chord changes.

A set of notes that belong together, played in order — the palette a song draws from. An ordered collection of notes spanning an octave, defined by its pattern of whole and half steps (e.g. the major scale: W-W-H-W-W-W-H). Melodies and bass lines are mostly built from a scale's notes.

Several notes that sound at the same time — the harmony the band plays over. Three or more notes sounded together, named by their root and quality (C, Cm, Cmaj7…). Bassists rarely play full chords, but every bass line outlines the current chord's notes.
A chord played one note at a time. The notes of a chord played in sequence instead of together. The bread and butter of bass lines: root, third, fifth, (seventh), one after another.

How fast the music goes — the speed of the beat. The speed of the underlying pulse, measured in beats per minute (bpm). 60 bpm is one beat per second; 120 bpm is two. Practice metronomes count it out loud.
The steady pulse you tap your foot to. The regular pulse underlying music. Beats group into bars (in most rock/pop, four beats per bar), and the bass usually locks tightly to them with the drums.

One small box of musical time — usually four beats long. A group of beats, marked off in written music with vertical lines. The time signature says how many beats each bar holds. Charts are read bar by bar.

The two stacked numbers that say how beats are grouped — like 4/4. A fraction-looking symbol at the start of written music: the top number is how many beats per bar, the bottom is which note value counts as one beat. 4/4 (four quarter-note beats per bar) is so common it's called "common time".

The rhythmic feel that makes people nod their heads. The repeating rhythmic pattern and feel of a piece — where notes land, how long they last, which ones are stressed. For a bassist, playing "in the pocket" (locked with the drums, steady and intentional) matters more than playing many notes.

Bass-specific notation: four lines for the four strings, numbers for the frets. Short for tablature. Each horizontal line is a string (bottom line = low E), each number is a fret to press (0 = open string). Tab shows you where; standard notation shows you what and when — this site teaches both, side by side.

Adjusting each string until it sounds its correct note. Setting each string's pitch with the tuning machines on the headstock. Standard bass tuning is E1–A1–D2–G2, low to high. Basses drift out of tune with temperature and playing — checking takes seconds with a tuner.

A device that clicks a perfectly steady beat for you to practice with. A tool that sounds the beat at an exact tempo. Practicing with one builds the timing that bandmates rely on from a bassist. This site's labs include one, and practice exercises give metronome targets.

A short, repeated musical phrase — the hook a song is built on. A short repeating figure, often played by bass and guitar together, that defines a song's identity. Learning riffs trains both hands and your ear at once.
say BASE clef. The fancy F at the start of a staff. Its two dots surround the line where F sits — that's how you anchor every other note. Bass music lives in this clef.
say TREB-el clef. The swirly symbol used for higher instruments (guitar, vocals, piano right hand). You'll see it on other people's charts — bassists read the bass clef.
say staff. The five horizontal lines music is written on. Higher on the staff = higher pitch. Notes sit on lines or in the spaces between them.
say bar line. A vertical line that marks off one bar (one group of beats). A double bar with a thick line means the end.
say flat. Lower the note by one semitone — one fret down toward the headstock. B♭ is one fret below B.
say sharp. Raise the note by one semitone — one fret up toward the body. F♯ is one fret above F.
say NATCH-er-al. Cancel a sharp or flat — play the plain letter-name note.
say whole note. A note held for four beats — a whole bar of 4/4. Written as an empty oval with no stem.
say half note. Two beats. An empty oval with a stem.
say QUOR-ter note. One beat — the foot-tap. A filled-in oval with a stem. Four of them fill a bar of 4/4.
say eighth note. Half a beat. A filled oval with a stem and one flag; groups get beamed together. Count them "1 & 2 &…".
say six-TEENTH note. A quarter of a beat — funk lives here. Two flags or a double beam. Count "1 e & a 2 e & a…".
say QUOR-ter rest. One beat of deliberate silence. Rests are played — muting on time is a core bass skill.
say dotted note. Adds half the note's own length. A dotted quarter = one and a half beats.
say four four. Four quarter-note beats per bar — the time signature of most rock, pop, and funk. So common it's also written 𝄴 ("common time").
say quarter equals ninety-two. The metronome speed: 92 quarter-note beats per minute. Practice exercises on this site give these as start and target tempos.
say tab. Four lines = the four strings (bottom line = low E string), numbers = which fret to press, 0 = open string. Shows where to play; the notation above it shows what and when.
say root. In this site's fretboard diagrams: the filled amber dot marks the root — the home note the shape is built on.
say fingers one to four. Left-hand fingering: 1 = index, 2 = middle, 3 = ring, 4 = pinky. (Thumb is T, rare on bass fretboards.)
